headline: Echoblast — Wikipedia article as of 2030-12-31
rendered_text:
# Echoblast

*From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia*

**Echoblast, Inc.** is an American cloud computing company specializing in [GPU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit) rental and managed [artificial intelligence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence) workloads.[1] The company operates a hybrid infrastructure model combining owned datacenter capacity with a third-party capacity marketplace, offering self-service on-demand GPU rental to individual developers and enterprise customers.[2] Echoblast is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and was founded in January 2025 as part of [Y Combinator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_Combinator)'s Winter 2025 batch.[3] The company went public on the [NASDAQ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NASDAQ) Global Select Market in Q1 2030 under the ticker symbol EBLA.[4]

| **Type** | Public company |
|---|---|
| **Traded as** | NASDAQ: EBLA |
| **Industry** | Cloud computing, GPU infrastructure, AI services |
| **Founded** | January 2025 |
| **Founders** | Will Coleman, Mark Howell, Angela Holmes, Mark Leonard |
| **Headquarters** | 500 Sansome Street, San Francisco, CA 94111 |
| **Key people** | Will Coleman (CEO), Mark Howell (CTO), Angela Holmes (President), Mark Leonard (Chief Architect) |
| **Products** | Echoblast Cloud, Echoblast Train, Echoblast Inference |
| **Website** | echoblast.ai |

## History

### Founding and Y Combinator (2025)

Echoblast was incorporated as a Delaware C-corporation in January 2025 and accepted into Y Combinator's Winter 2025 cohort.[5] The company was founded by Will Coleman (CEO), Mark Howell (CTO), Angela Holmes (President), and Mark Leonard (Chief Architect).[6] The founding team identified a market gap in GPU infrastructure provisioning, observing that existing cloud providers ([Amazon Web Services](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amazon_Web_Services), [Google Cloud Platform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google_Cloud_Platform), and [Microsoft Azure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Azure)) maintained high prices and lengthy procurement cycles for specialized hardware, while smaller developers and research teams lacked reliable access to cost-effective compute capacity.[7]

The company's initial strategy focused on building owned datacenter capacity while simultaneously constructing a marketplace layer to aggregate third-party GPU availability from regional providers and alternative infrastructure operators.[8] This dual-track approach was intended to balance gross margins from owned fleet operations with marketplace revenue and to reduce customer lock-in risk through infrastructure diversification.

### Early growth and Series A (2025–2027)

Following Y Combinator Demo Day in March 2025, Echoblast raised an initial seed round and began datacenter site acquisition and GPU procurement.[9] The company's first owned datacenter facility, designated EBL-US1, came online in late 2025 in Northern California.[10] Early customers included machine learning researchers, open-source model developers, and small AI startups seeking per-hour GPU rental without long-term commitments.[11]

In 2026, Echoblast announced its Series A funding round and launched Echoblast Train, a managed platform for distributed model training with integrated job orchestration, checkpoint management, and multi-node synchronization primitives.[12] The Train offering bundled GPU capacity with software services, targeting teams training large language models and computer vision systems.[13] The same year, the company expanded its marketplace to include capacity from over 40 regional datacenter partners across North America and Europe.[14]

### Expansion and product diversification (2027–2029)

By 2027, Echoblast had established a second owned datacenter facility, EBL-US2, in the Pacific Northwest.[15] The company also launched Echoblast Inference, a managed inference platform offering containerized model serving, auto-scaling, and multi-tenant isolation for production deployments.[16] Inference complemented the Train offering and allowed customers to transition models from training to production within the Echoblast ecosystem.[17]

In 2028, Echoblast announced Series B funding and began international expansion.[18] The company opened a European datacenter facility, EBL-EU1, in Frankfurt, Germany, in early 2029, addressing demand from EU-based customers and compliance requirements under the [General Data Protection Regulation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Data_Protection_Regulation) (GDPR).[19] The same period saw the addition of engineering offices in New York City and Seattle to expand hiring and customer support capabilities.[20]

By late 2029, Echoblast's marketplace had grown to include over 150 third-party capacity partners, and the company had begun negotiations with institutional investors regarding a potential public offering.[21]

### Initial Public Offering (2030)

Echoblast filed for an initial public offering in Q4 2029 and went public on the NASDAQ Global Select Market in Q1 2030 under the ticker symbol EBLA.[22] The IPO valued the company at approximately $3.2 billion.[23] The company's S-1 filing disclosed three reportable business segments: Cloud (on-demand and marketplace GPU rental), Train (managed training platform), and Inference (managed inference platform).[24]

Following the IPO, Echoblast accelerated datacenter expansion and increased research and development spending on platform reliability, multi-region orchestration, and cost optimization.[25] The company also announced a strategic partnership with a major cloud infrastructure provider to expand marketplace capacity in underserved regions.[26]

## Products and Services

### Echoblast Cloud

Echoblast Cloud is the company's core infrastructure offering, providing on-demand GPU rental via self-service portal.[27] Customers can provision individual or clustered GPU instances with configurable memory, interconnect bandwidth, and storage options.[28] The service supports both reserved capacity (with discounted rates and availability guarantees) and on-demand spot pricing.[29] Echoblast Cloud integrates with popular machine learning frameworks including [PyTorch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PyTorch), [TensorFlow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TensorFlow), and [JAX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JAX_(machine_learning)).

The Cloud segment operates on a hybrid model: a portion of capacity derives from Echoblast-owned datacenters, while a significant portion is sourced through the company's third-party marketplace.[30] The marketplace aggregates GPU availability from regional datacenter operators, allowing Echoblast to offer geographic diversity and reduce dependency on single infrastructure providers.[31]

### Echoblast Train

Echoblast Train is a managed platform for distributed machine learning model training.[32] The service provides automated job orchestration, distributed training coordination, checkpoint and logging management, and integration with popular model repositories and dataset platforms.[33] Train abstracts away infrastructure complexity, allowing teams to submit training jobs and specify resource requirements without managing individual instances or networking configuration.[34]

Train is designed for teams training large-scale models, including large language models and multimodal systems.[35] The platform supports custom training loops via containerized code submission and includes built-in support for popular training frameworks and distributed training libraries.[36]

### Echoblast Inference

Echoblast Inference, launched in 2027, provides managed model serving and inference infrastructure.[37] The platform handles model containerization, auto-scaling based on request load, batching optimization, and multi-tenant isolation.[38] Inference integrates with Echoblast Train, allowing models trained on the platform to be deployed to production with minimal configuration changes.[39]

## Corporate Affairs

### Leadership

As of the snapshot date, Echoblast's executive team consists of:

- **Will Coleman**, Chief Executive Officer and co-founder, responsible for overall company strategy and investor relations.[40]
- **Mark Howell**, Chief Technology Officer and co-founder, overseeing platform architecture and engineering organization.[41]
- **Angela Holmes**, President and co-founder, leading business development, sales, and customer success.[42]
- **Mark Leonard**, Chief Architect and co-founder, directing infrastructure architecture and long-term technical strategy.[43]

### Board of Directors

Following the 2030 IPO, Echoblast's board of directors includes the four co-founders plus independent directors appointed during the public company registration process.[44] As of December 2030, the board comprises seven members, including representatives from major institutional investors and independent technology industry veterans.[45]

## Data Center Footprint

As of December 2030, Echoblast operates or has announced the following owned datacenter facilities:

- **EBL-US1** (Northern California): Operational since late 2025; primary facility for North American customer workloads.[46]
- **EBL-US2** (Pacific Northwest): Operational since 2027; supports US West Coast and international customer traffic.[47]
- **EBL-EU1** (Frankfurt, Germany): Operational since early 2029; serves European customers and provides GDPR-compliant data residency.[48]

Additionally, Echoblast maintains office locations in San Francisco (headquarters), New York City (engineering and sales), Seattle (engineering and operations), and Amsterdam (EU sales and customer success).[49] The company's marketplace integration extends to over 150 third-party datacenter partners globally, allowing customers to provision capacity across diverse geographic and infrastructure provider options.[50]

## Criticism and Incidents

In 2029, Echoblast faced criticism from environmental advocacy groups regarding power consumption and cooling requirements at its Northern California datacenter facility.[51] The company responded by announcing a commitment to source 100% renewable energy for all owned facilities by 2032 and published a detailed sustainability roadmap.[52]

In mid-2030, following the company's IPO, short-seller reports questioned the company's marketplace unit economics and alleged that third-party capacity sourcing masked lower utilization of owned fleet assets.[53] Echoblast management disputed the report and published detailed financial disclosures in a subsequent SEC filing, noting that the hybrid model was intentional to reduce customer lock-in and provide geographic flexibility.[54]
